skill.name: hig-technologies
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines guidance for designing integrations with Apple technologies, including Siri, Apple Pay, HealthKit, HomeKit, ARKit, machine learning, iCloud, Sign in with Apple, SharePlay, CarPlay, Game Center, Wallet, NFC, Maps, VoiceOver, Mac Catalyst, and related platform services.
skill.summary: Design Apple technology integrations using focused Human Interface Guidelines checklists.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, cryptography, C2, and environment-access patterns, but manual review found Apple HIG Markdown reference content rather than executable behavior. Backticks are API symbol formatting, URLs point to Apple documentation and image assets, and the env_access hit is an image line, so the critical heuristic is dismissed. Residual risk is low because this community skill contains many external links that users or agents may reference.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links in Reference Markdown
low_findings.description: The skill includes many hardcoded URLs to Apple Developer documentation, Apple asset hosts, videos, and the author site. These are documentation references, not automatic network requests, but marketplace users should treat external links as untrusted destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are direct Markdown URLs. They do not execute code, but they are real outbound references in community-provided content.

low_findings.title: Backticked API Names Misclassified as Shell Commands
low_findings.description: The external_commands findings are false positives caused by Markdown code formatting around Apple API names and properties. No shell runner, script file, or user-controlled command execution path was found in the reviewed skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks wrap API identifiers inside prose and links. The files are Markdown references, not executable Ruby or shell code.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The env_access finding at the Tap to Pay reference points to an Apple image link, not a configuration library or secret access. No evidence found of environment variable reads, credential harvesting, or secret exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a Markdown image URL for an incorrect-usage illustration. It contains no environment access API or credential handling behavior.

content.value_statement: Apple platform integrations require careful HIG decisions across privacy, payments, health, voice, maps, and shared experiences. This skill turns Apple guidance into focused checklists, flows, and review criteria.
actual_capabilities: Creates implementation checklists for Apple technology integrations.
Summarizes required and optional HIG behaviors for platform services.
Maps privacy, permission, and consent requirements to user-facing flows.
Reviews payment, health, identity, and Wallet experiences for trust issues.
Provides testing guidance for edge cases, accessibility, and platform support.
Cross-references related Apple technologies when an integration spans services.
limitations: It is design guidance, not a substitute for Apple Developer documentation.
It does not grant entitlements or verify App Store approval requirements.
It may not reflect Apple changes after the bundled reference files were created.
It does not implement platform APIs or generate production-ready app code by itself.

use_cases.title: Plan a Payment Flow
use_cases.description: Define Apple Pay, Tap to Pay, or in-app purchase screens with clear pricing, fallback states, and user trust requirements.

use_cases.title: Review Sensitive Permissions
use_cases.description: Check HealthKit, Wallet identity, HomeKit, or AI features for consent, data minimization, and clear purpose strings.

use_cases.title: Prepare Platform QA
use_cases.description: Turn HIG guidance into acceptance criteria for Siri, CarPlay, Maps, VoiceOver, SharePlay, and Mac Catalyst experiences.

prompt_templates.title: Get a Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for one Apple technology.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an HIG checklist for integrating [Apple technology] in a [platform] app. Include required behaviors, optional improvements, privacy notes, and testing scenarios.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of an existing user journey.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this [Apple technology] flow against the HIG. Identify user trust issues, missing permission explanations, unclear states, and accessibility gaps: [flow description].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Implementation Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced product decision across multiple services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare [Apple technology A] and [Apple technology B] for this use case: [use case]. Recommend the HIG-aligned approach, required user controls, and fallback behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create Launch Criteria
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced readiness review before implementation or release.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the HIG guidance for [Apple technology] into launch criteria for design, engineering, privacy, accessibility, and QA. Include pass or fail review questions.

output_examples.input: Review an Apple Pay checkout for a marketplace app.
output: Checklist covering Apple Pay prominence, end-merchant disclosure, line item clarity, account creation timing, error handling, and confirmation states.
Risk notes for confusing handoffs, hidden fees, missing privacy statements, and weak order confirmation language.

output_examples.input: Plan HealthKit access for a fitness coaching feature.
output: Permission flow that asks only when needed, explains the health benefit, minimizes requested data, and supports refusal gracefully.
Testing guidance for denied access, partial data sharing, accessibility, and clear recovery paths.

output_examples.input: Create a Siri integration checklist for a task app.
output: Checklist for supported intents, natural phrases, fast completion, voice-only fallback, error recovery, and safe handling of consequential actions.
Review questions for custom vocabulary, App Shortcuts, confirmation language, and redundant app branding.

best_practices: Name the exact Apple technology, platform, and user goal before asking for guidance.
Provide the current flow or screen behavior so the skill can review real decisions.
Validate final implementation details against current Apple Developer documentation.
anti_patterns: Do not treat HIG guidance as legal, payment compliance, or entitlement approval advice.
Do not request broad sensitive permissions before users understand the benefit.
Do not copy Apple visual assets or button treatments without checking current usage rules.

faq.question: Which Apple technologies does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Siri, Apple Pay, HealthKit, HomeKit, ARKit, machine learning, iCloud, Sign in with Apple, SharePlay, CarPlay, Wallet, NFC, Maps, VoiceOver, and more.

faq.question: Can it replace Apple Developer documentation?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes bundled HIG references and helps structure decisions. Always verify API details and current requirements with Apple.

faq.question: Does it generate production code?
faq.answer: No. It provides design checklists, flow guidance, review criteria, and testing considerations for Apple technology integrations.

faq.question: Is it useful for privacy-sensitive features?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights permission timing, purpose strings, data minimization, consent, and user control for sensitive Apple services.

faq.question: Can it help with App Store approval?
faq.answer: It can help align interface decisions with HIG expectations, but it cannot guarantee approval or confirm entitlement eligibility.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level low?
faq.answer: The skill is Markdown guidance with many external links. Static command and environment findings were reviewed as documentation false positives.
